message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To examine the association between financial incentives from entry into a vaccine competition with the probability of vaccination for COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A cross-sectional study with adjustment for covariates using logistic regression</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>October and November 2021, Australia.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>2,375 respondents of the Taking the Pulse of the Nation Survey</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary and secondary outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The proportion of respondents who had any vaccination, a first dose only, or second dose after the competition opened.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Those who entered the competition were 2.27 (95% CI 1.73 to 2.99) times more likely to be vaccinated after the competition opened on October 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    than those who did not enter—an increase in the probability of having any dose of 0.16 (95 % CI 0.10 to 0.21) percentage points. This increase was mostly driven by those receiving second doses. Entrants were 2.39 (95% CI 1.80 to 3.17) times more likely to receive their second dose after the competition opened.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Those who entered the Million Dollar Vax competition were more likely to receive a vaccination after the competition opened compared to those who did not enter the competition, with this effect dominated by those receiving second doses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We use a nationally representative sample of individual self-reported vaccination status and timings.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We distinguish between the association between competition entry and first and second doses.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We adjust for a rich set of individual characteristics associated with vaccination status, and examine the factors influencing competition entry</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The strong association for second dose vaccinations may reflect some individuals who had already scheduled their second dose after the competition opened, potentially leading to an overestimate of the association.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: An observational study of the association between COVID-19 vaccination rates and entry into the Australian ‘Million Dollar Vax’ competition

reference.article-title: Association Between Statewide Financial Incentive Programs and COVID-19 Vaccination Rates
